student_message: A ladybug! Fold it straight down the middle.
final_message: Spot on! A ladybug folded straight down the middle is a perfect example. Now, let's look at a brand new kind of symmetry together called rotational symmetry. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated, or turned, around its center point by just a fraction of a full turn, like a $$1/4$$ turn or a $$1/2$$ turn. If it only looks the same after a complete spin, it doesn't count! You can even draw a little dot on the shape to track where it goes as you turn it. Did you know a starfish is a living math puzzle? ⭐ Because of rotational symmetry, if you spin a starfish, it looks exactly the same five different times before making a full circle! What other cool everyday object can you think of that spins and looks perfectly the same? 🤔

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce rotational symmetry and re-engage visual attention.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The section is not_started, so the primary agent must introduce the new concept. The student is happy but has been looking away for 7 consecutive turns, so the engagement agent is needed to guide her visual focus back to the lesson.

current_section_content: A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated (turned) by a fraction of a full turn around its centre point. The fractions we check are 1/4 turn, 1/2 turn, and 3/4 turn. A shape that only looks the same after a full turn does not count as having rotational symmetry. You can track this by placing a dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn.
session_summary: The session covered the concept of symmetry and lines of symmetry. The student understood the material well, successfully identifying a vertical line of symmetry on their own body. However, the student struggled significantly with focus and attention, frequently becoming distracted by off-topic subjects like their dog and favorite color. The student's current learning state reflects a solid grasp of basic symmetry, though they require constant redirection from the tutor to stay on task.
